content.problemStatement:
In the magical land of Byteland, there are three kinds of citizens:


a Bit - 2  ms2\;\mathrm{ms}2ms after a Bit appears, it grows up and becomes a Nibble (i.e. it disappears and a Nibble appears)
a Nibble - 8  ms8\;\mathrm{ms}8ms after a Nibble appears, it grows up and becomes a Byte
a Byte - 16  ms16\;\mathrm{ms}16ms after a Byte appears, it grows up, splits into two Bits and disappears
Chef wants to know the answer to the following question: what would the population of Byteland be immediately before the time N  msN\;\mathrm{ms}Nms if only 1 Bit appeared at time 0  ms0\;\mathrm{ms}0ms?

Help him and find the population (number of citizens) of each type.
content.inputFormat: The first line of the input contains a single integer T denoting the number of test cases. The description of T test cases follows. The first and only line of each test case contains a single integer N.
content.outputFormat: For each test case, print a single line containing three space-separated integers — the number of Bits, Nibbles and Bytes.

sampleTestCases.explanation: Immediately before the time 2ms, there is only one Bit. At 2ms, this Bit grows up, so immediately before 3ms, there is only one Nibble in Byteland.
At 16ms, the Byte splits into two Bits, so immediately before 16ms, there are two Bits in Byteland.

solution.approach: To solve this problem, we can simulate the process of Bits, Nibbles, and Bytes growing up and splitting. We start with one Bit at time 0 and then simulate the process for N milliseconds. At each millisecond, we check if any Bits, Nibbles, or Bytes are ready to grow up or split and update their counts accordingly.
solution.timeComplexity: O(T * N) — we simulate the process for N milliseconds for each of the T test cases
solution.spaceComplexity: O(1) — we only use a constant amount of space to store the counts of Bits, Nibbles, and Bytes
